subject_profile.summary: Base country-level assessment; default category weights prioritize rule of law, institutional integrity, and personal freedom.
stated_values: Rule of law and institutional integrity weighted most heavily
Personal freedom and security from arbitrary state action are foundational
Economic success is contingent on rule of law, not a substitute for it

flags: Generated 2026-05-28 under v2.0: multilateral + independent (IMF, World Bank, UN/OCHA/UNHCR, Freedom House, RSF, ICG, Soufan Center, CTC West Point, FEWS NET) + international press.
Data limitation: official statistics are weak and patchy, with most figures external (World Bank/IMF) estimates and large areas under al-Shabaab parallel governance; confidence is capped accordingly.

Standout features: a rare debt bright spot (the 2023 HIPC completion cut external debt below 6% of GDP on an IMF program), against a 2025 al-Shabaab resurgence, an AUSSOM funding crisis, clan fragmentation, and recurrent drought/famine.

economic.notes: A post-HIPC debt bright spot and dollarization-anchored inflation, against a thin, aid/remittance-dependent base.

fiscal_state.notes: Aid- and remittance-dependent with a narrow revenue base, but post-HIPC debt is under 6% of GDP and an IMF ECF anchors reform; 2025 donor-aid cuts squeeze the budget. (World Bank 2025; IMF)

monetary_independence.notes: Effectively no functioning national currency - heavy dollarization leaves the central bank with minimal tools. (World Bank)

reserve_currency_intl_monetary.notes: The shilling is not a reserve currency; the US dollar dominates transactions. (definitional)

inflation.notes: ~3.7% in 2025, relatively contained as dollarization imports US price discipline. (World Bank 2025)

banking_stability.notes: A thin, largely informal mobile-money/hawala system; formal banking is shallow and reintegrating post-debt-relief. (World Bank)

trade_actions_capacity.notes: A small livestock/agriculture export economy reliant on remittances and imports, vulnerable to drought and aid shocks. (World Bank 2025)

institutional.notes: Limited territorial control, collapsed-then-rebuilding data, and a deadly press environment.

rule_of_law.notes: The federal government has limited territorial control, with large areas under al-Shabaab parallel governance or clan authority; 'Not Free'. (Freedom House 2025)

statistical_integrity.notes: Weak and patchy - most figures are external estimates, though improving under the IMF program. (World Bank)

civil_service_capacity.notes: Limited reach beyond Mogadishu and donor-dependent, with federal-member-state friction. (CTC West Point)

press_freedom.notes: Journalists face arrest, violence, and killings from both state security and al-Shabaab. (Freedom House 2025; SJS)

political_social.notes: A 2025 insurgent resurgence and clan fragmentation over a conflict-displaced society.

political_stability.notes: A 2025 al-Shabaab resurgence encircling approaches to Mogadishu, federal-state fragmentation, and the Somaliland question make stability acutely fragile. (Soufan Center; ICG)

civil_liberties.notes: Severely constrained by conflict, al-Shabaab repression in held areas, and state pressure; 'Not Free'. (Freedom House 2025)

treatment_non_citizens.notes: Hosts some refugees/returnees but is overwhelmingly an origin/IDP country amid conflict and drought. (UNHCR/OCHA)

social_cohesion.notes: Clan-based politics and 4.5 power-sharing create chronic fragmentation that al-Shabaab exploits. (ICG; CTC)

demographics.notes: Very young and high-fertility, but with a human-capital deficit from weak services and conflict. (UN/World Bank)

geopolitical.notes: Internationally backed but dependent on a funding-strapped AU mission, with twin insurgencies.

alliance_reliability.notes: Recognised with Turkey/Gulf/US/AU partners (the 2024 Ankara reconciliation with Ethiopia), but dependent on fragile external security funding (the AUSSOM crisis). (UN; ISS)

conflict_involvement.notes: An active al-Shabaab insurgency, an ISIS-Somalia presence in Puntland, and the Ethiopia-Somaliland MoU dispute. (Soufan Center; CTC; ICG)

sanctions_capital_controls.notes: Targeted UN/US measures relate mainly to al-Shabaab/charcoal, not the state; reintegrating post-HIPC. (UN; World Bank)

physical_practical.notes: A national drought emergency, a near-collapsed health system, and high insecurity.

climate.notes: A November 2025 national drought emergency after failed rains; recurrent drought/flood cycles and famine risk. (OCHA 2025; WFP)

healthcare.notes: Among the world's weakest - very high maternal/child mortality and ~1.8M children facing acute malnutrition. (UNICEF/WHO)

infrastructure.notes: Poor and war-damaged, with pockets of port/Mogadishu recovery. (World Bank)

crime_safety.notes: High insecurity from insurgency, terrorism, checkpoints, and kidnapping. (ICG; ACLED-cited)

disaster_insurance.notes: Negligible market - risk borne by households/aid. (inference)

summary: A fragile federal state whose government controls only part of its territory, with a 2025 al-Shabaab offensive reversing prior gains and pushing the insurgency back toward Mogadishu while ISIS-Somalia holds out in Puntland. Security depends on the AU's AUSSOM mission, in acute funding crisis after the UN failed to secure assessed financing by mid-2025. The one clear bright spot is debt: the 2023 HIPC completion slashed external debt below 6% of GDP and an IMF program anchors reform, with dollarization keeping inflation relatively contained. But recurrent drought (a November 2025 national emergency), famine-level malnutrition, clan fragmentation, and federal-state friction keep the picture clearly negative; most figures rely on external estimation.
